reforms.description: The Japanese government has announced a reform package aimed at enhancing the efficiency of public institutions and increasing the number of start-ups from 140,000 to 240,000. This includes requiring special public institutions to raise funds on financial markets and creating a more supportive environment for start-ups, including easier financing from public financial institutions.
reforms.component_name: Public Institution Efficiency and Startup Support
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: The reform aims to foster innovation and entrepreneurship, which are key drivers of economic growth.
reforms.status_evidence: The reform has been officially announced but is not yet implemented.
reforms.importance_rationale: The reform represents a significant structural change in how public institutions operate and supports the startup ecosystem.
reforms.source_quote: The government has set an objective to increase the number of start-ups from 140,000 at present to 240,000 mainly by creating an appropriate environment including easier financing from public financial institutions.

reforms.description: The budgetary allocation for science and technology was significantly boosted as part of a five-year plan to strengthen basic R&D capacity, with a total allocation of 17.2 trillion yen aimed at increasing Japan's R&D share of GDP towards the OECD average.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: Increasing funding for basic R&D is essential for fostering innovation and long-term economic growth.
reforms.status_evidence: The budgetary allocation has been successfully implemented.
reforms.importance_rationale: The reform represents a major increase in public investment in R&D, which is critical for enhancing Japan's innovation capacity.
reforms.source_quote: Following the Science and Technology Basic Plan (decided in 1996), the government allocated 17.2 trillion yen in total to R&D for five years up to FY2000.

reforms.description: The Japanese government has implemented a new law granting 100% patent rights to private companies for government-sponsored projects, thereby encouraging innovation and R&D activities in the private sector.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: By enhancing patent rights for private companies, the reform incentivizes R&D and innovation, which can lead to economic growth.
reforms.status_evidence: The law has been successfully implemented.
reforms.importance_rationale: While significant, the reform is more of a regulatory adjustment rather than a structural overhaul.
reforms.source_quote: MITI has applied this principle to all the projects it has granted since the implementation of the law (1111 cases in FY1999 covering expenditures of 256 billion yen).
